title: What is the ROI of AI in business in 2026? An honest UK evidence review
excerpt: AI ROI is real, but concentrated. The honest UK evidence in 2026: 54% of firms now use AI, only 6% see meaningful enterprise EBIT impact, and the productivity story splits sharply between people who've actually used AI and people who haven't. A practical read for UK leaders.
content:
Last reviewed: 2026-04-29

AI ROI is real, but it's concentrated. Roughly 6% of organisations see enterprise-wide EBIT impact of 5% or more from AI; about 60% see no enterprise impact at all (McKinsey 2025). UK adoption hit 54% of firms in 2026, up from 23% in 2023, and current users expect +71pp of productivity gain over the next year (BCC March 2026). The wins cluster at 10 to 20% cost reductions in software engineering, IT and manufacturing, almost all function-level rather than company-wide.

What does AI ROI mean for a UK business in 2026?

AI ROI isn't a single number. The published evidence splits into three categories, and adding them together is where most business cases come unstuck.

Function-level ROI is cost or time saved on a specific workflow: contact-centre handling time, code review throughput, marketing brief turnaround. The easiest to measure, the easiest to win, and the strongest published figures sit here.

Enterprise-level ROI is earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) impact at the company level, traced back to AI use. Much harder. McKinsey's State of AI 2025 found only 6% of respondents (the AI high-performers) report 5% or more EBIT impact attributable to AI. Roughly 60% see no impact at all.

Hidden ROI is the third category: avoided costs, retention improvements, decisions made better. Real but speculative. Useful as a tie-breaker between options, not as the load-bearing justification for a business case.

The classic UK SME mistake is to muddle these together. A function-level proof point gets modelled as if it'll deliver enterprise EBIT. The function-level win is usually real. The journey from there to a number that shows up in the P&L is where the published failure rate lives.

What returns are UK businesses actually seeing?

The honest 2026 answer is that UK adoption has run miles ahead of UK measurement. The most recent BCC and University of Essex study, surveying 668 UK firms in March 2026, shows 54% of UK firms now use AI, up from 23% in 2023, 25% in 2024 and 35% in 2025. Near-tripled in three years.

Here's the bit that really matters, though. Firms already using AI expect a +71pp productivity gain over the next twelve months. Firms still planning it: +46pp. The fence-sitters: +26pp. The "never touching it" crowd: -3pp. So the people closest to the technology are the most optimistic about what it can do, and the people furthest from it are the least. The gap is statistically real (p<0.001 in the BCC dataset), not just enthusiasm bias.

What UK businesses are actually getting from AI in 2026, source by source:

Source Headline finding Sample / method Date
BCC and University of Essex 54% of UK firms use AI; current users expect +71pp net productivity gain over 12 months n=668 UK firms March 2026
BCC bespoke vs generic AI users 21% of bespoke-AI users report headcount decrease vs 3% of generic-AI-only users n=61 bespoke, n=265 generic-only; Fisher's exact p<0.001 March 2026
McKinsey State of AI Only 6% of organisations report ≥5% enterprise EBIT impact from AI; 60% see no enterprise impact Global executive survey March 2025
McKinsey by function 10–20% cost reductions in software engineering, manufacturing and IT; revenue uplift >10% in marketing/sales, strategy/finance, product/services Function-specific cuts of same survey March 2025
MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy Software developers self-reported 20% productivity uplift from AI tools; controlled measurement found a 20% productivity decrease (Metr study) Randomised study of experienced developers 2024
PwC / UK government modelling Full UK AI adoption could add £232 billion to the UK economy by 2030 and lift productivity from 0.9% to 1.5% per year Economic modelling cited in the AI Opportunities Action Plan Reaffirmed April 2026
BCC headcount impact 95% of UK firms using AI report no change to workforce size in the past 12 months; 86% report no change to job roles Same n=668 BCC sample March 2026

Two things sit awkwardly side by side here. UK adoption is now mainstream. Enterprise EBIT impact is rare. The honest reading is that most UK firms are getting real function-level value from AI but haven't restructured the business around it enough for that value to show up in the company P&L. McKinsey's published ratio is £5 of people investment for every £1 of AI technology spend. Most UK SMEs are nowhere near that.

The BCC data shows another useful split: firms using bespoke AI (custom integrations, RAG systems, fine-tuned models) versus firms using only generic tools (per-seat ChatGPT or Copilot subscriptions). Bespoke users were nearly seven times more likely to report a workforce decrease over the past twelve months. 21% of the 61 firms using bespoke AI in the BCC sample, against 3% of the 265 firms using generic tools only (Fisher's exact p<0.001).

The reading is straightforward. Generic productivity AI spreads small time savings across many people, which rarely shows up as a headcount change. Bespoke AI deployed against a specific high-volume workflow substitutes for staff time, which sometimes does. Whether that's a virtue or a risk is a leadership call. Both reflect real ROI, just measured differently.

How much does AI cost a UK SME?

The sterling reality, based on published vendor pricing in April 2026.

  • Per-seat productivity AI: £18 to £50 per user per month for ChatGPT Pro, Claude Pro and Microsoft 365 Copilot. The published list price for Microsoft 365 Copilot for business is £24.70 per user per month. Enterprise tiers can run higher once you negotiate.
  • Customer-service or knowledge-base bots on off-the-shelf platforms: £200 to £2,000 per month plus implementation. Build cost typically £5,000 to £40,000 depending on how many systems they need to talk to.
  • Bespoke retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) on private data: £25,000 to £200,000 to design and deploy, plus £500 to £5,000 a month for the model and infrastructure to keep it running.
  • Custom AI agent or fine-tuned model: £50,000 to £500,000 to build, with running costs that scale with usage.
  • The people line: McKinsey's published guidance is roughly £5 of people investment for every £1 of AI technology spend. Most UK SMEs are spending pennies on the pound.

The biggest mistake is to put a £25-per-user-per-month tool and a £150,000 bespoke deployment in the same business case as if they're the same kind of investment. They aren't. Per-seat tools deliver function-level uplift if used, and almost nothing if ignored. Bespoke deployments deliver enterprise-level uplift if integrated, redesigned around and adopted, and a writedown if not.

How long is the payback period?

The published payback ranges, by category:

  1. Per-seat productivity AI: 3 to 9 months in well-adopted teams. Payback hinges almost entirely on whether usage is real. UK firms tracking active-user rates rather than licence counts tend to land at the lower end. Firms that buy seats and never check rarely see payback at all.
  2. Off-the-shelf bots and assistants for specific functions: 6 to 18 months. The wide range is the price of integration: where the bot connects to clean data and a clear workflow, payback is fast; where it sits on top of legacy data with conflicting sources, it's slow.
  3. Bespoke deployments and custom agents: 12 to 36 months. The function-level win usually shows up in year one (sometimes year two). Enterprise EBIT impact, where it appears at all, lands in years two and three.

One counter-finding worth keeping in mind, though. The Metr study of experienced software developers, summarised by MIT's Initiative on the Digital Economy, found that participants self-reported a 20% productivity uplift from AI coding tools while measurement of actual task completion showed they were 20% slower. Self-reported productivity isn't productivity. Any UK leader signing off an AI investment should be requiring objective measurement, not survey questions to staff about how much faster they feel.

How should a UK leader measure AI ROI honestly?

Five things to do, in this order, if you're a UK leader assessing or running an AI investment in 2026.

  1. Measure outcomes, not adoption. Track active usage by individual, completion rates on the workflows AI is meant to support, error rates, and customer or staff satisfaction. Licences purchased is a vanity metric.
  2. Separate function-level claims from enterprise-level claims. If your business case promises enterprise EBIT impact, ask honestly which of the published 6% you expect to be in. If it's a function-level productivity claim, present it as one and protect it from over-promising.
  3. Test for measurement honesty. If your only ROI evidence is staff saying they feel more productive, build in objective measurement before you scale. The Metr finding is the canonical warning.
  4. Budget the people line at scale. McKinsey's £5-to-£1 ratio is a useful upper bound, but even half that is more than most UK SMEs have allocated. Training, role redesign and change management are the difference between function-level wins and enterprise impact.
  5. Set a 12-month review gate. Decide the criteria for continue, expand, or stop on each AI investment before you start. Review against the BCC adoption-versus-impact gap honestly: 95% of UK firms using AI saw no headcount change in the past 12 months. If your case promised one, the absence is information.

The honest 2026 position is that AI ROI is available, but it isn't free. The published evidence rewards firms that adopt deliberately, measure objectively, invest in the people line alongside the technology line, and accept that enterprise-level impact is rare and earned. Firms that buy licences and hope for the best are paying for a productivity boost their staff feel but don't actually get.

About the author

Tom McCaul is the founder of Tom & Co, an AI agency advising UK businesses on adoption, governance and content strategy. He works directly with senior teams on AI deployment that delivers measurable function-level ROI and earns enterprise-level impact over time. Connect on LinkedIn.
